stem: Find the coefficient of \( x^{[[n]]} \) in the Maclaurin series of \( f(x) = \dfrac{1}{(1-[[a]]x)(1-[[b]]x)} \).
distractor_rationales: This is the coefficient of \( x^{[[n]]-1} \) (off by one in the exponent).
This multiplies the two geometric ratios, giving \( ([[a]]\cdot[[b]])^{[[n]]} \).
This adds the individual \( n \)th powers instead of forming the correct sum \( a^n + a^{n-1}b + \cdots + b^n \).
This keeps only the \( [[a]] \) geometric series, ignoring the \( [[b]] \) factor.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.

stem: Find the coefficient of \( x^{[[n]]} \) in the Maclaurin series of \( f(x) = (1 + [[a]]x)\,e^{[[b]]x} \), where \( [[n]] \ge 1 \).
solution: The series for \( e^{[[b]]x} \) has coefficient \( \dfrac{[[b]]^{k}}{k!} \) on \( x^{k} \). Multiplying by \( (1+[[a]]x) \) (a Cauchy product with only two terms) contributes the \( x^{[[n]]} \) term from \( 1\cdot\dfrac{[[b]]^{[[n]]}}{[[n]]!}x^{[[n]]} \) and from \( [[a]]x\cdot\dfrac{[[b]]^{[[n]]-1}}{([[n]]-1)!}x^{[[n]]-1} \), giving \( \dfrac{[[b]]^{[[n]]}}{[[n]]!} + \dfrac{[[a]]\,[[b]]^{[[n]]-1}}{([[n]]-1)!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the contribution from the constant term \( 1 \), ignoring the \( [[a]]x \) term entirely.
This keeps only the contribution from the \( [[a]]x \) term, ignoring the constant term \( 1 \).
This wrongly adds \( a \) and \( b \) before applying the exponential series, rather than doing a Cauchy product.
This multiplies both contributions together instead of adding two separate product terms.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.

stem: Find the coefficient of \( x^{2\cdot[[n]]} \) in the Maclaurin series of \( f(x) = e^{[[a]]x^{2}} \).
solution: Substitute \( u = [[a]]x^{2} \) into \( e^{u} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{u^{k}}{k!} \), giving \( e^{[[a]]x^{2}} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{[[a]]^{k}}{k!}x^{2k} \). Setting \( k=[[n]] \), the coefficient of \( x^{2\cdot[[n]]} \) is \( \dfrac{[[a]]^{[[n]]}}{[[n]]!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( [[n]]! \), the factorial that comes from the exponential series.
This uses \( a^{2n} \) instead of \( a^{n} \), confusing the exponent of \( a \) with the exponent of \( x \).
This divides by \( (2n)! \) instead of \( n! \), confusing the power of \( x \) with the series index \( k \).
This is the coefficient of \( x^{2([[n]]-1)} \) (off by one in the series index).
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.

stem: Find the coefficient of \( x^{3\cdot[[n]]} \) in the Maclaurin series of \( f(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 - [[a]]x^{3}} \).
solution: Substitute \( u = [[a]]x^{3} \) into \( \dfrac{1}{1-u} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}u^{k} \), giving \( \dfrac{1}{1-[[a]]x^{3}} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}[[a]]^{k}x^{3k} \) (all other powers of \( x \) have coefficient \( 0 \)). Setting \( k=[[n]] \), the coefficient of \( x^{3\cdot[[n]]} \) is \( [[a]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( a^{3n} \) instead of \( a^{n} \), confusing the exponent of \( a \) with the exponent of \( x \).
This wrongly divides by \( n! \), a mistake carried over from series like \( e^{u} \) that don't apply to a geometric series.
This multiplies by the exponent \( 3 \) of \( x^3 \) instead of using it only to identify which power of \( x \) is nonzero.
This is the coefficient of \( x^{3([[n]]-1)} \) (off by one in the series index).
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
